data.title: Paychex: If Shares Keep Falling, An Upgrade Could Be In Store
data.description:
In early October of last year, I came very close to upgrading Paychex (PAYX) from a "Hold" to a "Buy." This was based on strong financial performance, driven in part by the company's successful integration of Paycor HCM, which is a player in the workforce management solutions space that Paychex acquired in a deal valued at $4.1 billion. Thanks in large part to that purchase, revenue, profits, and cash flows were all growing nicely. And the expectation was that 2026 would be a great year for the firm. The stock was also becoming cheaper than it had been in the past. But it wasn't cheap enough, in my view, to justify a bullish outlook.

Since that time, the stock has fallen significantly, plunging 20.7%, while the S&P 500 is up 3.3%. That certainly makes me glad I didn't pull the trigger on an upgrade back then. Now, however, things are looking a bit different. Admittedly, financial performance for the business has been mixed as of late. But with revenue expanding nicely and shares trading at more reasonable levels, an upgrade is tempting. The only thing stopping me at this time is the fact that, relative to firms that have similarities to it, the stock is not exactly cheap. In fact, right now, it sits pretty squarely in the fair value camp. Unfortunately, this means that I am not going to upgrade it right now. But if the stock falls further, or if fundamentals show strong signs of improvement, an upgrade could be around the corner.

Taking a fresh look at Paychex

Thanks in large part to the acquisition of Paycor HCM, Paychex is doing an exceptional job from a revenue standpoint. The newest data that investors have access to covers through the second quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Revenue for that time amounted to $1.56 billion. That's a sizable increase over the $1.32 billion the company reported a year earlier. This rise in sales was driven largely by the Management Solutions segment of the company. This is the part of the company that offers up HCM solutions like those that help to facilitate payroll, to aid in HR management, to facilitate talent acquisition and management, to administer benefits, and more. It even engages in certain activities like the facilitation of retirement solutions. Examples here include 401(k) plans, IRAs, and the like.

Management reported that of the 21.1% increase that revenue saw during the quarter compared to the same time a year earlier, about 17% was because of the company's purchase of Paycor HCM that was completed in April of last year. The rest was organic in nature, with the company chalking the expansion up to a growth in the number of clients served and a rise in client worksite employees under the HR solutions category. Higher prices and increased product penetration also helped. Meanwhile, the PEO and Insurance Solutions portion of the company grew more modestly, with revenue expanding from $317.9 million to $336.9 million. This is the part of the business that offers workforce solutions where it still retains employment over the individuals in question. And it also offers up insurance through different carriers centered around business owner policies, casualty coverage for workers compensation, commercial automotive coverage, dental and vision plans, and more. Management attributed this increase to the number of worksite employees, as well as to greater insurance revenue.

I wish I could say that overall profitability for the company had improved. But it didn't. Net income actually dropped from $413.4 million to $395.4 million. The good news was that some of this was one-time in nature. This included, as an example, $77.1 million of acquisition-related costs. On an adjusted basis, net profits for the company actually jumped from $411 million to $454.6 million. Operating cash flow surged from $295 million to $444.9 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a decline from $554.6 million to $497.2 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business dropped from $579.1 million to $698.4 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first half of 2026 compared to the same time in 2025. Revenue increased nicely. And even though earnings were down, adjusted earnings and all cash flow metrics for the company improved. What is really exciting is that management said that 2026 should see adjusted earnings per share that would be between 10% and 11% above what the company saw in 2025. At the midpoint, we would be looking at an adjusted net income of about $1.99 billion. Adjusted operating cash flow seems to be on track to hit $2.05 billion, while EBITDA should expand to $3.01 billion.

Using these estimates, we can easily value the company. In the chart above, you can see exactly how the stock is priced at the moment. For a high-quality, service-oriented business, I would say that these multiples aren't bad. And in fact, even though the stock is more expensive than I would normally like, because of the nature of the business, it is tempting to take a more bullish stance on it. Having said that, relative to other companies that have similarities to it, the stock seems to be more in the fair value range. In that same chart, you can see exactly what I mean. Two of the five institutions that I compared it to are cheaper than it is on a price-to-earnings basis. And when it comes to the other two approaches to valuing the company, three of the five companies ended up being cheaper.

It does seem as though management disagrees with me regarding the valuation of the company. On Jan. 16 of this year, the company authorized a $1 billion share buyback program. The goal of this is to replace the 2024 authorization that gave the company the opportunity to buy $400 million worth of stock. Management has a good track record of returning capital to shareholders. Between dividends and share buybacks, it returned $1.5 billion in cash to investors during the 2025 fiscal year alone. And in the first half of the 2026 fiscal year, the company repurchased 2.1 million shares in exchange for $286.6 million. This was in addition to the $777 million worth of dividends paid out.

Even though I am taking a more neutral stance on the company, this does not mean that I am anything other than optimistic about it from an operational standpoint. Currently, the company plays in a massive space. According to it, there are about 136 million private sector employees in the U.S. today. Its services touch on about 13 million of these. The overall target market of the company is for employers with between 1 and 5,000 employees. That total market size is estimated to be about 86 million employees. That gives the company a 15% market share. When it comes to the total HCM market, the estimated value today stands at over $100 billion. This is a significant opportunity for the industry leader. And long-term, we should expect continued growth here.

Takeaway

As much as I would love to be bullish about Paychex, I just can't do that right now. Operationally, I think that the business will go on to thrive. But on a relative basis, the stock is really priced in the fair value range. On an absolute basis, it's a bit more attractive. But when you take all the data together, it definitely fits the fair value description more than it does the undervalued one. That justifies a "Hold" rating as opposed to a "Buy" rating.

data.title: Lumentum's Massive Run Now Faces A Reality Check
data.description:
The interesting part about Lumentum (LITE) right now isn’t that the AI optics business is growing strongly. Everyone can see that. What’s interesting is that Lumentum is starting to look like a capacity rationed utility in the AI buildout while the stock trades like a growth stock that can’t possibly fail.

The quarter was real, the guide is real, and it’s legitimately loud. Revenue came in at $665.5 million for the fiscal second quarter and the company guided fiscal third quarter revenue of $780 to $830 million with the operating margin rising again. This is the kind of growth that makes customers stop negotiating and start asking for allocations. The language out of the call was telling: sold out fabs, allocations, LTAs, price step-ups, and customers asking for more than contracted. It’s a very different narrative from the typical optical component narrative.

But I also don’t want to confuse a great business opportunity with a great entry point. I think Lumentum is being priced as a stock that can’t possibly fail, not as a stock that’s a key supplier. And that’s because at $48 billion of equity value, Lumentum is not priced as a key supplier but as the chokepoint.

The Real AI Bottleneck Isn’t GPUs, It’s Indium Phosphide

If I had to distill this all down to a single edge case, it is a question of indium phosphide supply with execution credibility behind it. Lumentum can be fully allocated and mean it. They’re shipping a record amount of EMLs, accelerating their 200G ramp ahead of plan and bringing forward a significant portion of their planned capacity expansions. And that’s important because we’re unit-constrained in the market, not share-constrained. And the current limiter in the market is not demand creation but demand allocation.

So why is all of this important? It’s why the Nvidia (NVDA) relationship is not just a PR headline for me. It’s a $2 billion investment alongside multi-year commitments to procure from Lumentum. In my mind, that’s a pretty clear indicator that the leaders in AI have shifted from a procurement conversation to a conversation about securing their supply chain. That is, in my opinion, Nvidia is treating advanced lasers and optics as a critical part of their infrastructure stack, not something you procure opportunistically and in a lot of ways, that changes their negotiation position subtly but materially.

CPO Is Inevitable But Timing Is Everything

I take management’s seriousness around CPO not being optional in the longer term seriously, and physics is a hard science that ultimately wins out. The transcript also has a nice distinction between scale-out and scale-up optics, and I like that they are positioning a world where optics incrementally takes more and more from copper’s territory.

I want to be clear that I am taking a conservative view on CPO because, let’s face it, it is one of those inevitabilities where the timing is always moving around a lot. The customer will continue to use copper for a lot longer than people want to admit if the reliability and servicing model is not bulletproof. The advantage that Lumentum has is that ultra-high-power reliability has been battle-tested in very harsh environments and that should be important as we move closer to the compute complex with optics. That said, I do not want to pay today for a potential architecture shift in 2027 to 2028 and assume that is locked in, so I want to view the CPO upside as optionality until unit volumes start to show up.

The Valuation Now Assumes Everything Goes Right

At the current price, the debate is no longer if LITE is a winner, it is how perfect is already embedded in the price. The market is essentially pricing in a clean road to $10+ in earnings potential in fiscal 2027 and in addition to that, a multiple premium because this is the bottleneck in the network. I am willing to pay a premium multiple for a supply chain leader with accelerating margins, LTAs and strategic relationships with customers. What I do not want to pay is a premium multiple without any air pockets in case anything goes wrong.

If 2027 comes in at $10 to $11 dollars of EPS, a 30 to 35 multiple can certainly be made, especially if OCS is scaling and 200G mix is increasing. The challenge is that the stock already acts as if it is priced for that scenario. What additional upside can we get? Either higher-than-consensus earnings or a market that believes a higher multiple expansion is warranted, and that’s a tougher bet after a $50 billion story. To me, that makes it an expectation-heavy and risky stock rather than a simple rich.

The Real Risk: Losing the Bottleneck

I don’t think the risk is competition. The risk is losing bottleneck control. If supply-demand pressures ease faster than we anticipate, either because competitors are stepping up or customers are designing around our constraints, then Lumentum’s fundamental advantages could disappear rapidly. Not a business killer but a story killer. Second, OCS is a timing landmine. If conversion into 2027 is down substantially, I would re-evaluate how sticky OCS really is versus how project-based it really is.

Third, watch the transceiver business for margin discipline. Management can claim all it wants about improving yields and increasing profitability at higher nodes but if systems growth ramps up faster than expected and starts to drag consolidated margins down, it would be punished for it because it’s currently being rewarded for increasing margins, not just increasing revenues.

Finally, if LTAs are losing negotiating leverage, whether through customer or spot price pressure, it would be a yellow flag to me because it would indicate that the seller’s market is normalizing.

Bottom line

I believe Lumentum to be the best-quality bellwether in the optics stack but at these valuation levels, I am not being compensated for ordinary execution. I would hold it if you own it and would look to add to it after a meaningful pullback. What would change my opinion would be clean conversion out of OCS and continued margin expansion through mid-2026.

data.title: AeroVironment Q4 Preview: The $1.4B Problem (Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

As military drone maker AeroVironment, Inc. (AVAV), or AV, gets ready to report Q3 FY26 earnings release ("ER") on March 10th, the company is sitting on another huge goldmine of opportunity for allied nations to deploy its drones.

The recent & sudden escalation of U.S.-Iran tensions will potentially lead to another surge in demand for loitering uncrewed munitions like drones that are routinely deployed in highly sensitive areas of heavy geopolitical conflicts, creating a huge opportunity for expanding AV’s order backlog, similar to the Ukraine conflict.

But AV faces headwinds from the possibility of a talk about losing either parts of or entire contract orders from different defense agencies, which will continue to put pressure on the drone maker’s shares in an interim period, keeping the outlook volatile for the near term.

I am downgrading my view on AeroVironment to Neutral ahead of fiscal Q3 earnings, explaining my thesis below.

More Contract Visibility Needed From AeroVironment

For military vendors like AV, who supply products like uncrewed munition weapons to the U.S. and its allied forces, stability and visibility in their backlog are extremely crucial in helping investors anchor their expectations of AV’s outlook. I had explained that in my thesis on AV many months ago when the company had won a billion-dollar contract from the U.S. Army. Until Jan. this year, my prior bullish thesis on AV perfectly played out, but since then, severe uncertainty has crept into AV’s outlook.

This billion-dollar contract was reported to be a mix of cost-plus and fixed-pricing contracts that I noted, at the time, would not be a major drag on AV’s gross margins. In many ways gross margins also help structure the forward visibility for the drone maker.

As AV gets ready to report its Q3 FY26 earnings next week, these 2 factors will be scrutinized by investors more than ever to shape the current investability in AV’s shares—order backlog growth and margin expansion.

Based on AV’s Q2 ER in December last year, AV reported a 134% y/y jump in the funded $1.1B part of AV’s backlog (that portion of AV’s backlog that has been approved and paid for by Congress, or DoW/Department of War, etc.). All of that jump in backlog has come during the fourth year of elevated geopolitical tensions, starting with the Russia/Ukraine war, Israel, and the recent Iran tensions. That surge in AV’s order backlog has been instrumental in drastically pulling up AV’s top-line growth from the mid-teens CAGR between FY20 and FY23 and the ~23% CAGR between FY23 and FY25.

AV is now a $1.4B revenue-generating business, with its Autonomous revenue segment doing most of the heavy lifting, accounting for ~60% of AV’s business. Sales of all of AV’s uncrewed aircraft systems/drones, such as the Switchblade 300/600 and the Puma drones. The Autonomous segment also pulls up AV’s entire gross margin profile as seen in the Q2 FY26 scoreboard below, where Q2 margins have decelerated to 27% due to a meaningful contraction in product gross margin.

Space, Cyber, and Directed Energy, or SCDE, represents AV’s other revenue segment, which oversees the sales of space uncrewed systems and solutions as well as services for cyber intelligence and mission systems, and is the other emerging segment for AV and is mainly anchored by its acquisition of BlueHalo.

While BlueHalo brings the promise of pushing AV towards a new growth vector, the full integration of BlueHalo’s assets and operations into AV has been a strong drag on AV’s margin profile. A year after announcing BlueHalo’s acquisition, the integration has been completed with an unfortunate steep drag down in AV’s margins, slashing it by half from the 40% level seen in this new world of elevated geo-tensions to under 20% after the complete integration of BlueHalo.

In the upcoming Q3 ER, I anticipate the market's focus will be entirely on AV’s gross margin results more than the top-line growth or any other metric. That’s because management has explained that almost all of its margin headwind has come from integrating the BlueHalo acquisition and AV’s ancillary services revenues, which are, as I highlighted earlier, a low-teens margin business. Management has clearly stated expectations of gross margins beginning to inflate starting in Q3, especially in Q4. Their expectations for gross margin inflection starting in Q3 stem from the belief that product revenues, the higher-margin segment, will start ramping faster in Q3, supported by the delivery of the BADGER program (Broad Area Deployable Ground terminal) and the LOCUST program. This ramp up in product revenues should see quarterly gross margins begin its ascent back up to the “high 30s by Q4.”

The risk to AV is the constant back and forth in the narrative surrounding SCAR (Satellite Communication Augmentation Resource), the U.S. Space Force’s $1.4B originally awarded to BlueHalo but has since witnessed volatile progress. The BADGER program that AV is working on is a key component of the SCAR program.

Earlier this year, the US government issued a stop work order to AV for the delivery of BADGER antenna systems under the SCAR program. In March, the SCAR contract was reportedly reopened but altered to allow for a competitive bidding process in contrast to the period before the stop work order when AV’s BlueHalo was the sole contract vendor of the SCAR program.

AV’s management has since explained that they “remain in active negotiations with the U.S. Space Force regarding AV’s contract to deliver ground stations to support the SCAR program.” But the uncertainty around the contract and the sheer dollar volume of the $1.4B contract present too much risk for markets to be optimistic on AV, leading to a pressured outlook so far on AV.

Heading into Q3 FY26 ER this week, the risk/reward profile is elevated. If management does announce winning the entire $1.4B contract, it represents significant & immediate potential to more than double AV’s backlog well beyond the $1.1B funded backlog it has today. But the likelihood of that scenario of winning the entire $1.4B contract has diminished from earlier this year given the slate of events since the start of the year at the US government’s end, creating uncertainty.

Markets will look towards management for more clarity on contract visibility and any updated impact(s) to its gross margin profile.

AeroVironment’s Valuation Levels Signal A Move In Both Directions

As AV heads towards Q3 ER, its shares trade at a forward revenue multiple of 5.8x FY26 revenues of 1.97B, growing ~4x. But in terms of FY27 expected revenues worth $2.3B, growing just 17%, AV trades at ~4.9x FY27 revenues, which is rich compared to its FY26 forward revenue multiple.

In my view, there is a fair bit of uncertainty in both these revenue multiples. Especially in terms of FY27 revenue multiples, which I believe could climb to >7x if AV ends up winning the entire $1.4B SCAR contract or could crash to under <3x if AV loses the entire SCAR contract.

This is why I have downgraded my view on AV until the uncertainty clears out.

Risks & Other Factors To Note

The numbers markets will be watching in the Q3 ER on March 10 next week are the following:

  • Q3 FY26 expectations: 69 cents EPS on $475.7M in revenues (+183% y/y). Also, increasing gross margins must be demonstrated.
  • Q4 expectations: 179 cents EPS on $566M in revenues (+106% y/y). Also, high 30s gross margins must be demonstrated.
  • FY26 & FY27 revenue growth expectations: 139% in FY26 and 17% in FY27.
  • There will be questions about how Middle East tensions rising from Iran could be a catalyst for AV, but investors must look through management’s answers to understand how those responses shape the backlog growth and margin expansion outlook.

Takeaway

The risk/reward profile for AeroVironment’s shares looks skewed towards the risk side as AeroVironment gears up for Q3 FY26 earnings this week.

Simmering geopolitical tensions arising from Iran and the Middle East could give AeroVironment a lift, but the company needs to come clear on how backlog growth will expand, supplemented by a clear path towards gross margin expansion back up to the thirties level.

Due to heightened uncertainty, I am downgrading AeroVironment’s shares to a Hold ahead of earnings.

data.title: HP: Hardware Weakness Is Creating A Double-Digit Yield Opportunity
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered HP Inc. (HPQ), I called them a “Severely Mispriced Cash Flow Powerhouse,” upgrading them to a Strong Buy while highlighting their strong cash flow, clear undervaluation and cost savings while adapting to the near- and medium-term macro pressure.

With the price getting more attractive despite a solid report recently, the yield and upside potential are even more impressive now, with HPQ remaining in a very solid position to take advantage of a recovery in the industry once the current headwinds are over.

Internal Developments

Although outweighed by their mixed outlook, HPQ reported a strong Q1’FY26, with a 6.9% increase in revenue, as the strong 11.1% growth in personal systems significantly offsetting the 2.2% drop in printing, while the Non-GAAP operating margin also stood at 6.9%.

HPQ remains on track to meet the $1 billion gross annualized run rate savings by the end of FY28 announced recently, although given the ongoing CEO transition, I'd wait to see an update about the new CEO's plans during their Investor Day, which was delayed from April following the sudden CEO change.

As for their FY26 outlook, HPQ continues to see about $2.8 billion to $3 billion in FCF (expected to be at the low end of the range), while expecting their revenue to grow faster than the market and moderating in the second half of the fiscal year, advancing on the cost savings I mentioned before and proving to be quite resilient despite the ongoing hardware supply-demand pressure.

Financially, based on HPQ’s latest report, we continue to see an overall solid position, with current assets below current liabilities due to how the business operates, while the $8.84 billion in debt remains manageable and well-spread over a long period, while they still have about $3.15 billion worth of cash and cash equivalents, providing some financial flexibility if needed.

As mentioned in the previous analysis, HPQ increased their dividend by 4%, currently standing at a ~6.26% dividend yield that costs about $277 million (in Q1’FY26), alongside $325 million on buybacks, which gets to an annualized ~$2.4 billion, meaning a combined yield of ~13.68%, for a payout ratio of ~82.75% based on their midpoint FCF guidance.

Given the strong cash flow and valuation, I’d strongly push for even more buybacks (even going as far as cutting the dividend for it), as this temporary weakness caused by the macro pressures I’ll mention below could be an excellent opportunity for the company to reduce their number of shares significantly.

Macro Developments

To begin with the ongoing attack against Iran, this remains a very big source of pressure and uncertainty, with oil/gas prices surging and Iran closing the Strait of Hormuz, which is key for international trade in oil, gas, fertilizers, etc., potentially leading to broader economic issues.

Although Trump said that the US Navy could escort ships, big shipping companies like Maersk (AMKBY) didn’t want to take that kind of risk, and not only did they avoid it, but also the entire region instead (including the Suez Canal), preferring to take the long Cape of Good Hope route, potentially resulting in significant delays/extra costs in global trade.

Beyond that, depending on how long this conflict will last (which I believe is entirely up to the end goal), we could even see more attacks against countries like Saudi Arabia or the UAE (where a couple of AWS (AMZN) centers were recently hit by drones), which are involved in many AI/tech/data center investments.

If the focus there suddenly shifts to increasing their military/defense capacity or they simply decide to cut back on AI spending due to the broader macro environment, we could see this as a headwind for the confidence that fueled significant gains in the stock market, although it can actually be beneficial for HPQ given the potential normalization of the hardware market.

The tariff situation can also help following the Supreme Court’s decision, although they were subsequently lifted to 15%, which HPQ’s new CEO (as the previous one went to PayPal (PYPL) not long ago, which I also rated a Strong Buy recently) said during their Q1'FY26 Earnings Call would not negatively impact them, although I would add that the situation continues to be uncertain and can always shift again:

We are evaluating the impact of the decisions including the announcement of new tariffs in the last few days. Right now, we do not expect to be negatively impacted by the subsequent developments following the court decision. However, we will continue to engage the administration on these matters and others. We are confident in the strength and agility of our supply chain, which provides the flexibility needed to navigate an uncertain trade-related cost environment.

However, don’t expect these hardware headwinds to fix overnight, as it can take months or even years for these issues to normalize, while the consumer environment continues to be under pressure, with some of the worst levels of US consumer confidence since 2014 seen recently despite some improvements and significant trade-downs even for the most basic things such as food, so delaying a laptop purchase for example at current prices doesn’t seem like such an irrational thing to do.

Still, I believe these delays are only a matter of time, as hardware will inevitably have to be replaced and HPQ should stand to benefit from the next cycle, regardless of its timing, while the company remains in a strong position from many points of view and still manages to prove their resilience. The company’s CEO also talked about this during their Q1’FY26 Earnings Call, which also highlighted their mitigation efforts, including long-term supply chain agreements (memory requirements for FY26 are covered), expanding lower-cost commodity sourcing and other supply/cost strategies:

Like others, we are seeing increased input costs driven primarily by the rising prices of DRAM and NAND. We expect this volatility to remain throughout fiscal '26 and likely into fiscal '27. While we believe the market will rationalize over time, we have already been implementing a number of mitigation measures.

This is similar to other expectations and is in line with my own as well, with IDC forecasting an 11.3% drop for the global PC market in 2026, with the market flattening in 2027 and a rebound from 2028 onwards. These couple of years of significant pressure will likely be a good time to accumulate HPQ, while the company’s cost savings and strong cash flow can be used for significant buybacks.

Risks Reiterated

Despite some improvements in terms of tariffs and rates for HPQ and its clients/partners/consumers, the macro landscape continues to be under pressure, and I don’t expect the hardware issues to be solved overnight, while the sudden CEO transition can add another layer of uncertainty that the market doesn’t appreciate.

Given the environment, it also means that it’s more difficult for HPQ to pass these extra costs to the buyer, as the ongoing macro pressure already affected the affordability of their products. Although that’s normal in a high-rate environment, this pressure is accentuated by several other macro worries.

Valuation

For HPQ’s DCF to Equity, starting with the lowest end of the FCF guidance of $2.8 billion in FY26, without accounting for net borrowing changes as I’ll consider the current level of debt to be maintained, highlighting their “pure” cash flow potential.

I’ll go for a more conservative $2.6 billion in FY27 given the potential to see them being more exposed to macro pressure (offset by cost savings), followed by a conservative return to $2.9 billion in FY28 (this year’s midpoint guidance) and a 4.5% CAGR until FY31 thanks to a modest recovery despite their significant potential to benefit from macro improvements that can support a new PC replacement cycle and buybacks helping the per share results, followed by a 3.5% CAGR for FY32 to FY36, with a 2.5% terminal growth rate and a high 12% discount rate to take into account the ongoing pressure and risks, with 932 million shares outstanding as of their Q1’FY26 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $30.88 billion, for an intrinsic value of $33.14 per share, which is significantly above the current $19.34 despite using relatively conservative estimates, while more realistic ones get us in the $40s.

Regardless of how careful we are with the valuation assumptions, the current level is way too conservative, and the upside from here remains very hard to ignore. For the sake of the example, assuming a massive 15% discount rate and no step-up in 2028 (just a 4.5% increase instead), we get to an intrinsic value of $23.67, still above current levels.

Conclusions

HPQ remains a Strong Buy, backed by a very attractive valuation, strong cash flow and long-term macro tailwinds despite near- and medium-term pressure, all while rewarding a strong yield to wait.

There are risks, especially given all the ongoing macro uncertainty, potential for more weakness and longer-than-expected hardware issues and CEO transition risks, but the upside for long-term investors when the current problems are over remains very significant, in my opinion.

data.title: Teradyne's Rerating Is Real, But The Next Leg Depends On AI Test Intensity
data.description:
I wrote about Teradyne (TER) before. At the time, I pointed to the AI compute and networking as segments driving revenue growth, while also functioning as margin expanders. At the time, my chart analysis pointed towards a breakout on the back of this underlying trend. That setup worked very well, with the stock re-rating massively and never looking back.

Several months later, the stock still seems to be discounting the company's earning power trajectory. The company went from a cycle business to AI infra leverage in a hurry. This is the market realizing the company is no longer a mobile seasonality story, and it is becoming a "test and automation" layer in the AI infra buildout.

Where Is The Revenue Coming From?

A look at the revenue shows us that AI compute is becoming the center of the business. In the past, the company was highly dependent on mobile phones, and the underlying cycle. That was how the market saw the company in that long sideways range. The market was still seeing the observable baggage: cyclical ATE and lumpiness in the customer spending. And rightly so, test markets are concentrated and there is a limited number of clients driving purchases. That is exactly the kind of setup where Wyckoff's "composite operator" can accumulate without the stock going anywhere. Lots of supply to absorb with no consensus on a new narrative.

However, that is changing. The company is now a bet on AI CapEx, thanks to the growth in compute product revenue going from $109M in 2023 to $753 in 2025. That's a tectonic shift in the business model and led the change in perception around the stock.

Teradyne handed the market further proof. With Sales around $1.08 billion, operating profit 29% higher, and the company disclosing "over 60% AI-driven revenue" the skepticism just disappeared.

The Strategy That Makes The Model Work

The latest releases show that AI has become the main growth driver for the company. In this segment the UltraFLEXplus (FLEX test platform) appears as one of the main product lines. This moves the needle because compute testers are high ASP, with ramp concentrated in bursts, which ultimately leads to big quarterly delta in revenue. On the same token, as compute devices need more complex test patterns, test intensity rises and that leads automatically to more revenue.

Another interesting segment is the Magnum (memory test), with Magnum 7 designed for parallel memory testing, namely HBM. As memory has become a major bottleneck in the AI compute arena, and memory needs intensify, Teradyne has a major opportunity to derive more revenue from HBM and DRAM as final test content per bit is trending higher.

We can see that what moves the margin profile will ultimately be services attach. Things like extended warranties, training, and application support usually have higher gross margins, smoothing out the lumpy hardware shipment cycles. But services won't the only thing moving the needle on operating leverage. The mix shift toward high-end computing and memory configurations is likely the justification why the gross margin dipped somewhat from 59.4% to 57.2% while the operating margin expanded.

Valuation

If you look at the sales multiple for our comparative company selection, you will notice that the market is already pricing Teradyne as a premium AI infra semicap. The company trading near ASML's P/S is a bit revealing, given that the European company is usually regarded as a structurally advantaged gate keeper. For Teradyne to benefit from such a re-rate must mean that the market sees AI as a big driver for testing, and that wave will be big. Also, the company's position in compute, HBM and DRAM testing is strong. Finally, it is credible that the market is extrapolating that the company can achieve a higher steady state margin profile.

On the P/FCF side, the valuation seems more rich. But we have good reasons to believe that the market is just shrugging a depressed near term FCF and betting on a big jump in the following quarters. The justification is simple, in the business segment it is usual that EPS looks better than FCF during a ramp. The company needs build working capital (inventory), CapEx also steps up, and finally the generic trend of is always towards being consuming cash now, while generating later.

Risks

This thesis does not exist without a set of risks that are worth mapping. The AI-driven TAM expansion implies a surge that might lead to a digestion, and the market does not seem be pricing that. Even the company is telling us in the filings that the revenue is lumpy and we should not annualize a simply hot quarter. The market is pricing Teradyne as a premium AI infra play, but a digestion phase is likely to unleash a multiple compression period.

Additionally, the story also involves gaining share in compute and memory testing. We need to bear in mind that share in compute is not something owned, it is something to be contested in a highly concentrated customer base (top 5 customer represented 44% of revenue in 2025). The company can progress in absolute terms due to TAM expansion but numbers still look bad if the overall share remains low.

Margins might prove harder to improve than we currently consider due to difficulty in improving the premium mix and scaling OpEx efficiently. Margins stalling will likely mean that the EPS growth power story is done and the multiple will compress.

Finally, there are other fat tail risks that can break the multiple regardless of the management's ability to execute. Geopolitics comes to mind, but it is not the only exogenous risk.

Wrapping Up

Bringing this home, the key aspect here is that Teradyne is no longer being driven by a good smartphone year. The company is being perceived as a test and automation play in the AI buildout, and that gives you a very different earnings power profile.

The valuation is the reality check. Based on the consensus table, and peer valuation, we can see that the market is already putting a premium on the business. A look at the consensus table shows that the company is valued at 11.4x FY26 and 9.7x FY27, and that means a big chunk of the rate already happened.

The question becomes can Teradyne keep the premium multiple as the AI story remains the center of gravity? To answer this we need to consider the overall landscape for this type of company. In semicap land we generally have two buckets: one for structural gatekeepers that will hold 10x to 12x revenue multiples, and a second one for cyclical tools that will live closer to 6x and 8x revenues. This is a rough simplification, but in broad accordance to our previous peer valuation snapshot.

Therefore, considering 157M shares, the back of the envelope calculations become:

  1. bull case at 12x FY27 (12x$4.95B equals $59.4B in equity value or $378 price target);
  2. ii) the bear case at 8x FY27 (8x$4.95B equals $39.6 in equity value or $252 price target);

The risk return profile is almost symmetrical, and that basically tells us that there is room to run, but that we are no longer early to the re-rate. I rate it a buy, but I would not chase it, I would rather accumulate on dips (like the recent one) and be patient.
